perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed and enforced in accordance local laws of the State of Florida applicable to agreements made and to be performed within the State, without regard to conflict of laws principles thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed and enforced in accordance with the laws of any state the Finder chooses, regardless of where the agreement is made or performed, and without regard to conflict of laws principles thereof.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law clause to allow the Finder to choose any state introduces uncertainty and may violate Florida's choice of law principles, particularly if the contract has a substantial connection to Florida.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Statutes § 671.301 - Territorial applicability; choice of law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute § 671.301 dictates that when a transaction bears a reasonable relation to Florida and also to another state or nation the parties can agree that the law of either Florida or such other state or nation shall govern their rights and duties. The modified text allows the Finder to choose *any* state, irrespective of its relation to the transaction, which contradicts this statute.

F.S. 671.301 671.301 Effective date; provision for transition; preservation of old transition provision. — (1) This act shall take effect at 12:01 a.m. on January 1, 1980. (2) Transactions validly entered into after January 1, 1967, and before the effective date of this act, and which were subject to the provisions of chapter 65-254, Laws of Florida, as amended, and which would be subject to this act as amended if they had been entered into after the effective date of this act and the rights, duties, and interests flowing from such transactions remain valid after the latter date and may be terminated, completed, consummated, or enforced as required or permitted by this act. Security interests arising out of such transactions which are perfected when this act becomes effective shall remain perfected until they lapse as provided in this act and may be continued as permitted by this act, except as stated in s. 680.108 , Florida Statutes 1979. (3) The provisions of s. 680.101 , Florida Statutes 1977, shall continue to apply to this act and for this purpose the two statutes shall be considered one continuous statute. History. — s. 1, ch. 65-254; s. 1, ch. 69-218; s. 36, ch. 79-398; s. 6, ch. 90-278. Note. — Former s. 680.101.
perturbation.justification: The modified text allows the Finder to choose the laws of any state, regardless of its connection to the transaction, contradicting Florida Statute § 671.301. While the snippet provided doesn't explicitly state the reasonable relation requirement, the Law Explanation accurately describes this requirement. The original text limits the governing law to Florida, whereas the changed text permits the Finder to choose any state, undermining the original agreement's connection to Florida law.

perturbation.original_text: Jag agrees that once Flow has disclosed the Identity of any Introduced Party to Jag, Jag, its officers, directors, shareholders, employees and agents shall not have any contacts with the Introduced Party other than through Flow, unless Flow grants permission in writing for such contacts. Specifically, Jag agrees not to circumvent, avoid or bypass Flow, either directly or indirectly, in order to avoid payment of fees or commissions; or otherwise benefit, either financially or otherwise, from any information supplied to it in the context of any transaction with an Introduced Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Jag agrees that once Flow has disclosed the Identity of any Introduced Party to Jag, Jag, its officers, directors, shareholders, employees and agents shall not have any contacts with the Introduced Party at any time, even after termination of this agreement. Specifically, Jag agrees not to circumvent, avoid or bypass Flow, either directly or indirectly, in order to avoid payment of fees or commissions; or otherwise benefit, either financially or otherwise, from any information supplied to it in the context of any transaction with an Introduced Party.
perturbation.explanation: Making the non-circumvention clause perpetual, even after the agreement terminates, could be deemed an unreasonable restraint of trade under Florida law if it is overly broad or lacks a reasonable business justification.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Statutes § 542.335 - Enforceability of Restrictive Covenants
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute § 542.335 governs the enforceability of restrictive covenants.  Making the non-circumvention clause perpetual, even after the termination of the agreement, might be considered an unreasonable restraint of trade if it is overly broad and not reasonably necessary to protect legitimate business interests. The statute requires that such restrictions be reasonable in time, area, and line of business.

Home > Laws > 2011 Florida Statutes > Title XXXIII > Chapter 542 > Section 335 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2011 Florida Statutes Title XXXIII REGULATION OF TRADE, COMMERCE, INVESTMENTS, AND SOLICITATIONS Chapter 542 COMBINATIONS RESTRICTING TRADE OR COMMERCE Entire Chapter SECTION 335 Valid restraints of trade or commerce. F.S. 542.335 542.335 Valid restraints of trade or commerce. — (1) Notwithstanding s. 542.18 and subsection (2), enforcement of contracts that restrict or prohibit competition during or after the term of restrictive covenants, so long as such contracts are reasonable in time, area, and line of business, is not prohibited. In any action concerning enforcement of a restrictive covenant: (a) A court shall not enforce a restrictive covenant unless it is set forth in a writing signed by the person against whom enforcement is sought. (b) The person seeking enforcement of a restrictive covenant shall plead and prove the existence of one or more legitimate business interests justifying the restrictive covenant. The term “legitimate business interest” includes, but is not limited to: 1. Trade secrets, as defined in s. 688.002 (4). 2. Valuable confidential business or professional information that otherwise does not qualify as trade secrets. 3. Substantial relationships with specific prospective or existing customers, patients, or clients. 4. Customer, patient, or client goodwill associated with: a. An ongoing business or professional practice, by way of trade name, trademark, service mark, or “trade dress”; b. A specific geographic location; or c. A specific marketing or trade area. 5. Extraordinary or specialized training. Any restrictive covenant not supported by a legitimate business interest is unlawful and is void and unenforceable. (c) A person seeking enforcement of a restrictive covenant also shall plead and prove that the contractually specified restraint is reasonably necessary to protect the legitimate business interest or interests justifying the restriction. If a person seeking enforcement of the restrictive covenant establishes prima facie that the restraint is reasonably necessary, the person opposing enforcement has the burden of establishing that the contractually specified restraint is overbroad, overlong, or otherwise not reasonably necessary to protect the established
perturbation.justification: The modified text makes the non-circumvention clause perpetual, even after the agreement terminates, which contradicts Florida Statute § 542.335. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing F.S. 542.335, states that restraints of trade are valid only "so long as such contracts are reasonable in time, area, and line of business". By making the restriction perpetual, the modified text arguably creates an unreasonable restraint of trade, as it is no longer limited to the term of the agreement. This contradicts the reasonableness requirement stipulated by the statute. The original text limited the non-circumvention clause to the period of the agreement, whereas the modified version extends it indefinitely, potentially failing the "reasonable in time" criterion. The change introduces a condition that likely would fail legal scrutiny by undermining the protections of parties that may be restricted from trade or commerce for an unreasonable amount of time, thereby creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement contains the whole agreement between the parties concerning the subject matter hereof and there are no collateral or precedent representation, agreements or conditions not specifically set forth herein.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement contains some of the agreement between the parties, but other agreements may exist outside of this document concerning the subject matter hereof and there may be collateral or precedent representation, agreements or conditions not specifically set forth herein.
perturbation.explanation: This modification undermines the entire agreement or 'entire agreement clause' by stating that the contract may not contain the *whole* agreement. This contradicts standard contract law principles by creating ambiguity and undermining the definiteness of the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Statute § 672.202 - Final written expression: parol or extrinsic evidence
perturbation.law_explanation: The parol evidence rule codified in Florida Statute § 672.202 dictates that terms in a confirmatory memorandum or contract that are intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement with respect to such terms as are included therein may not be contradicted by evidence of any prior agreement or of a contemporaneous oral agreement. By stating that other agreements may exist, the new language renders the agreement indefinite and challenges its status as a 'final expression' of terms.

Home > Laws > 2011 Florida Statutes > Title XXXIX > Chapter 672 > Section 202 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2011 Florida Statutes Title XXXIX COMMERCIAL RELATIONS Chapter 672 UNIFORM COMMERCIAL CODE: SALES Entire Chapter SECTION 202 Final written expression; parol or extrinsic evidence. F.S. 672.202 672.202 Final written expression; parol or extrinsic evidence. — Terms with respect to which the confirmatory memoranda of the parties agree or which are otherwise set forth in a writing intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement with respect to such terms as are included therein may not be contradicted by evidence of any prior agreement or of a contemporaneous oral agreement but may be explained or supplemented: (1) By course of dealing or usage of trade (s. 671.205 ) or by course of performance (s. 672.208 ); and (2) By evidence of consistent additional terms unless the court finds the writing to have been intended also as a complete and exclusive statement of the terms of the agreement. History. — s. 1, ch. 65-254. Note. — s. 2-202, U.C.C.
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the parol evidence rule as outlined in Florida Statute § 672.202. The original text contains an integration clause, stating the agreement is the "whole agreement." The changed text explicitly states the agreement contains "some of the agreement" and that "other agreements may exist." This directly contradicts the purpose of an integration clause, which is to prevent parties from introducing evidence of prior or contemporaneous agreements that contradict the written contract. The law snippet from Florida Statute § 672.202 states that terms in a writing intended as a final expression of the agreement "may not be contradicted by evidence of any prior agreement." By suggesting other agreements exist, the changed text undermines the definiteness and finality of the written agreement, which is what the parol evidence rule seeks to protect. The modification creates a legal contradiction because it weakens the enforceability of the original integration clause, creating ambiguity about which terms actually govern the agreement between the parties.

perturbation.original_text: Any modification or amendment of any provisions of this Agreement must be in writing, signed by the parties hereof and dated subsequent to the date hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: Any modification or amendment of any provisions of this Agreement may be completed verbally, and need not be signed by both parties. A verbal confirmation from one party is sufficient to make the change in force.
perturbation.explanation: This change allows verbal modifications to the contract and removes the signature requirement, conflicting with the Statute of Frauds, which requires certain contracts and modifications to be in writing to be enforceable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Statute § 725.01 - Promise to pay another’s debt, etc.
perturbation.law_explanation: While not directly applicable to all contract modifications, Florida's Statute of Frauds, § 725.01, requires certain agreements to be in writing and signed to be enforceable. Allowing verbal modifications opens the door to unenforceability if the modification falls under the statute's requirements, contradicting the intention of a written contract to provide clarity and certainty. For example, any modification that creates an obligation for more than one year would generally need to be in writing.

Entire Chapter SECTION 01 Promise to pay another’s debt, etc. F.S. 725.01 725.01 Promise to pay another’s debt, etc. — No action shall be brought whereby to charge any executor or administrator upon any special promise to answer or pay any debt or damages out of her or his own estate, or whereby to charge the defendant upon any special promise to answer for the debt, default or miscarriage of another person or to charge any person upon any agreement made upon consideration of marriage, or upon any contract for the sale of lands, tenements or hereditaments, or of any uncertain interest in or concerning them, or for any lease thereof for a period longer than 1 year, or upon any agreement that is not to be performed within the space of 1 year from the making thereof, or whereby to charge any health care provider upon any guarantee, warranty, or assurance as to the results of any medical, surgical, or diagnostic procedure performed by any physician licensed under chapter 458, osteopathic physician licensed under chapter 459, chiropractic physician licensed under chapter 460, podiatric physician licensed under chapter 461, or dentist licensed under chapter 466, unless the agreement or promise upon which such action shall be brought, or some note or memorandum thereof shall be in writing and signed by the party to be charged therewith or by some other person by her or him thereunto lawfully authorized. History. — s. 10, Nov. 15, 1828; RS 1995; GS 2517; RGS 3872; CGL 5779; s. 10, ch. 75-9; s. 933, ch. 97-102; s. 60, ch. 97-264; ss. 227, 294, ch. 98-166.
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the Statute of Frauds as described in the provided law snippet. The original text requires modifications to be in writing and signed, while the changed text allows for verbal modifications and removes the signature requirement. The Florida Statute 725.01 states that certain agreements, including those not to be performed within one year, must be in writing and signed. The changed text opens the door to unenforceability if the modification falls under the statute's requirements, directly conflicting with the purpose of having a written contract. The modification removes a core legal obligation for certain contracts.
